request.question: When was the army whose soldiers Rameshwar Banerjee was trying to get released when he was shot formed?

title: Let's Go! (Wang Chung song)
Indian National Army
So Yours
Newburgh Conspiracy
A Lick of the Old Cassette Box
Cedric the Entertainer Presents
Rameshwar Banerjee
Nukleopatra
Byeolgigun
The 4th Floor (1999 film)
sentences: "Let's Go!"
is a single by Wang Chung.
Although the song was on their 1986 album, "Mosaic", it didn't get released as a single until January 6, 1987.
The single was a hit for Wang Chung in the United States, and it provided the band with their second (and so far, last) top-10 hit on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in April 1987, peaking at #9.
The single also formed the basis for a music video.
It also hit #9 on the Canada "RPM" Top 100 Singles chart.
The Indian National Army (INA; "Azad Hind Fauj" ; "lit."
: Free Indian Army) was an armed force formed by Indian nationalists in 1942 in Southeast Asia during World War II.
Its aim was to secure Indian independence from British rule.
It formed an alliance with Imperial Japan in the latter's campaign in the Southeast Asian theatre of WWII.
The army was first formed in 1942 under Mohan Singh, by Indian PoWs of the British-Indian Army captured by Japan in the Malayan campaign and at Singapore.
This first INA collapsed and was disbanded in December that year after differences between the INA leadership and the Japanese military over its role in Japan's war in Asia.
It was revived under the leadership of Subhas Chandra Bose after his arrival in Southeast Asia in 1943.
The army was declared to be the army of Bose's "Arzi Hukumat-e-Azad Hind" (the Provisional Government of Free India).
Under Bose's leadership, the INA drew ex-prisoners and thousands of civilian volunteers from the Indian expatriate population in Malaya (present-day Malaysia) and Burma.

This second INA fought along with the Imperial Japanese Army against the British and Commonwealth forces in the campaigns in Burma, in Imphal and at Kohima, and later against the successful Burma Campaign of the Allies.
"So Yours" is a song by Swedish singer and songwriter Darin.
It was released as a single on 22 February 2013 in Germany, Austria and Switzerland and all over Europe on 14 May.

This is Darin's third single to get released outside the Scandinavian area, after "Insanity" and "Desire" which were both released as singles, taken from the album "Break the News" in Germany, Austria and Switzerland in 2007.
The Newburgh Conspiracy was what appeared to be a planned military coup by the Continental Army in March 1783, when the American Revolutionary War was at its end.
Possibly instigated by political actors in the Congress of the Confederation, an anonymous letter was circulated in the army camp at Newburgh, New York, on March 10, 1783.

The letter suggested that the army, whose soldiers were discontented over pay that was in arrears and a lack of funding for promised pensions, should take unspecified action against Congress to resolve the issue.
The letter was written by Major John Armstrong, aide to General Horatio Gates, although the authorship of its text and underlying ideas is a subject of historical debate.

A Lick of the Old Cassette Box aka "The Lost Album" is an album by the British industrial rock/alternative dance band Pop Will Eat Itself originally recorded in 1995 and 1996, but not released until September 2013.
It was originally intended to become the band's sixth album back in 1996 but did not get released due to the band breaking up that year.
Cedric the Entertainer Presents was an American sketch comedy television series and television program featuring dance numbers starring Cedric the Entertainer.
The series premiered September 18, 2002 on Fox and after the first season, Fox renewed the show for a second season but right before the show aired its second season, Fox canceled the show.
The show did get released on DVD.
The complete series is available for purchase on sites such as Walmart and Target.
Rameshwar Banerjee (Bengali: রামেশ্বর বন্দ্যোপাধ্যায় ; 8 February, 1925 - 21 November, 1945) was a revolutionary and martyr of the Indian Independence Movement.
He participated in the Quit India Movement in 1942.
During the rally organized by the students against British rule and for release of the officers of Indian National Army, he was killed by police firing.
Nukleopatra is the sixth studio album from British dance music band Dead or Alive and last record to feature all-original content.
Still a massive success in Japan, Epic Records released "Nukleopatra" there in 1995.
Left without a record contract in the UK and the US, this album did not get released in other territories until years later.
"Nukleopatra" was eventually issued on several different labels, with varying track listings, timings and album artwork.
The Byeolgigun (Korean: 별기군, "Special Skills Force" or "Special Army") was the first modernised military force of Korea.
First conceived in 1876, it was formed in 1881 and trained by Japanese officers led by Horimoto Reijo, military attaché at the Japanese legation.
It received better treatment than the old Korean Army, whose soldiers' salaries were in arrears on account of the costs of the "Byeolgigun".
This led in 1882 to the Imo mutiny, in which soldiers rioted and Horimoto Reijo was killed.
The 4th Floor is a 1999 mystery horror thriller film, written and directed by Josh Klausner.
The film stars Juliette Lewis, William Hurt, Shelley Duvall and Austin Pendleton.
The film was released in 1999 in Germany, but didn't get released in the United States until 2000 when it went direct-to-video.
It was filmed on location in New York City and Saint John, New Brunswick, Canada.
The original music was written by Brian Tyler.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: When was the army whose soldiers Rameshwar Banerjee was trying to get released when he was shot formed?
hop1_docs: Rameshwar Banerjee | Rameshwar Banerjee (Bengali: রামেশ্বর বন্দ্যোপাধ্যায় ; 8 February, 1925 - 21 November, 1945) was a revolutionary and martyr of the Indian Independence Movement.  He participated in the Quit India Movement in 1942.  During the rally organized by the students against British rule and for release of the officers of Indian National Army, he was killed by police firing.

La Sciantosa | "La Sciantosa" is an Italian TV movie produced by RAI television.  (Even though, it was filmed in color, back in 1970, RAI's shows were still being shown in black and white, since they didn't convert to color TV until 1977.)  Anna Magnani played a faded "sciantosa" (i.e., variety show diva), Flora, whose career has declined.  When she receives an invitation to sing at an army base, she thinks she's singing to the front line soldiers, so she accepts the invitation.  She and her assistant, Cristina, played by Rosita Pisano, are met at the train station by Tonino, a young soldier, played by Massimo Ranieri.  He then drives the ladies to the army base, and then introduces them to his misfit band of musicians, including a very annoying horn player, who almost causes Flora not to want to sing for the soldiers, and a guitar player, who ruins a march song by blowing a kazoo at the end.  Flora's set to take the stage, draped in an Italian flag and wearing a crown.  But when the curtain opens, and she sees the severity of some of the soldiers' injuries, she gets instant stage fright.  After 2 unsuccessful tries to get her to do the march song, Tonino tells the band to play "'O surdato 'nnammurato", which Anna Magnani beautifully sings the song herself.  At that point, she has an epiphany and breaks down.  After the performance, the base is under attack, and Tonino helps Flora out of the building.  Unfortunately, Flora and Cristina get separated, and towards the end of the movie, Flora realizes how much she mistreated Cristina, and decides to sacrifice herself by shielding Tonino from an enemy plane firing at his car.

Mickey's Garden | Mickey's Garden is a 1935 American animated short film produced by Walt Disney Productions and released by United Artists.  The film was the second "Mickey Mouse" cartoon shot in Technicolor (after "The Band Concert") and is also the first color appearance of Pluto.  The cartoon is also the first color cartoon where Mickey speaks (though he only does so at the end when Pluto starts licking him).  The film's plot centers on Mickey Mouse trying to rid his garden of insects, but they keep coming back.  When he accidentally gets hit with his own bug spray, he begins seeing a warped reality.

Bloody Sunday (1972) | Bloody Sunday – sometimes called the Bogside Massacre – was an incident on 30 January 1972 in the Bogside area of Derry, Northern Ireland, when British soldiers shot 28 unarmed civilians during a peaceful protest march against internment.  Fourteen people died: thirteen were killed outright, while the death of another man four months later was attributed to his injuries.  Many of the victims were shot while fleeing from the soldiers and some were shot while trying to help the wounded.  Other protesters were injured by rubber bullets or batons, and two were run down by army vehicles.  The march had been organised by the Northern Ireland Civil Rights Association (NICRA).  The soldiers involved were members of the 1st Battalion, Parachute Regiment, also known as "1 Para".

The Recollections of Rifleman Harris | The Recollections of Rifleman Harris is a memoir published in 1848 of the experiences of an enlisted soldier in the 95th Regiment of Foot in the British Army during the Napoleonic Wars.  The eponymous soldier was Benjamin Randell Harris, a private who joined the regiment in 1803 and served in many of the early campaigns in the Peninsula War.  In the mid-1830s, Harris was working as a cobbler in London when he met an acquaintance, Captain Henry Curling, who asked him to dictate an account of his experiences of army life.  This account was then held by Curling until 1848, when he succeeded in getting the manuscript published, preserving one of the very few surviving accounts of military service in this era from a private soldier.

Sander Thoenes | Sander Thoenes (November 7, 1968 – September 21, 1999) was a Dutch journalist who was killed, near Dili in East Timor, by soldiers of Indonesian army.  He was shot when their paths crossed on a road as the Indonesians withdrew from the territory.

Thomas Hickey (soldier) | Thomas Hickey (hanged on June 28, 1776) was a Continental Army soldier in the American Revolutionary War, and the first person executed for treason against what would become the United States.  Born in Ireland, he came to America as a soldier in the British Army and fought as personal assistant to Major General William Johnson in the Seven Years' War, but deserted to the other side when the Revolution broke out.  He became part of the Life Guard, which protected Gen. George Washington, his staff and the Continental Army's payroll.  Hickey was jailed for passing counterfeit money in New York, tried and executed for mutiny and sedition, and he may have been involved in an assassination plot against George Washington in 1776.
response.summary_1: Passage [1]: rally demanded release of officers of the 'Indian National Army' (INA). Bridge: the formation date of the Indian National Army (when was the INA formed). Retrieval: 1) Open the 'Indian National Army' Wikipedia page and inspect the lead and 'History'/'Formation' sections for the founding/formation date; or 2) search "Indian National Army formed" site:wikipedia.org and check the cited sources.
hop2_docs: Indian National Army | The Indian National Army (INA; "Azad Hind Fauj" ; "lit." : Free Indian Army) was an armed force formed by Indian nationalists in 1942 in Southeast Asia during World War II.  Its aim was to secure Indian independence from British rule.  It formed an alliance with Imperial Japan in the latter's campaign in the Southeast Asian theatre of WWII.  The army was first formed in 1942 under Mohan Singh, by Indian PoWs of the British-Indian Army captured by Japan in the Malayan campaign and at Singapore.  This first INA collapsed and was disbanded in December that year after differences between the INA leadership and the Japanese military over its role in Japan's war in Asia.  It was revived under the leadership of Subhas Chandra Bose after his arrival in Southeast Asia in 1943.  The army was declared to be the army of Bose's "Arzi Hukumat-e-Azad Hind" (the Provisional Government of Free India).  Under Bose's leadership, the INA drew ex-prisoners and thousands of civilian volunteers from the Indian expatriate population in Malaya (present-day Malaysia) and Burma.  This second INA fought along with the Imperial Japanese Army against the British and Commonwealth forces in the campaigns in Burma, in Imphal and at Kohima, and later against the successful Burma Campaign of the Allies.

Subhas Brigade | The Subhas Brigade, or the 1st Guerrilla Regiment was a unit of the Indian National Army (INA).  The unit was formed in 1943 and unofficially referred to as Subhas Brigade after the Indian independence leader Subhas Chandra Bose, who at the time was also the supreme commander of the army.  The unit was the first and major commitment of the second INA in the Imphal Offensive, and along with Azad, Gandhi and Nehru Brigade, the Army's contribution to the Imperial Japanese Army's U-go offensive.

First Indian National Army | The First Indian National Army (or the First INA) was the Indian National Army as it existed between February and December 1942.  It was formed with Japanese aid and support after the Fall of Singapore and consisted of approximately 12,000 of the 40,000 Indian prisoners of war who were captured either during the Malayan campaign or surrendered at Singapore and was led by Mohan Singh.  It was formally proclaimed in April 1942 and declared the subordinate military wing of the Indian Independence League in June that year.  The unit was dissolved in December 1942 after apprehensions of Japanese motives with regards to the INA led to disagreements and distrust between Mohan Singh and INA leadership on one hand, and the League's leadership, most notably Rash Behari Bose.  A large number of the INAs initial volunteers, however, later went on to join the INA in its second incarnation under Subhas Chandra Bose.

Gandhi Brigade (regiment) | The Gandhi Brigade or the 2nd Guerrilla Regiment of the Indian National Army formed a part of the First INA and later formed a part of the 1st Division after its revival under Subhas Chandra Bose.

Rajputana Rifles | The Rajputana Rifles is the most senior rifle regiment of the Indian Army.  It was originally raised in 1921 as part of the British Indian Army, when six previously existing regiments were amalgamated to form six battalions of the 6th Rajputana Rifles.  In 1945 the numeral designation was dropped from the title and in 1947 the regiment was transferred to the newly independent Indian Army.  Since independence, the regiment has been involved in a number of conflicts against Pakistan, as well as contributing to the Custodian Force (India) in Korea under the aegis of the United Nations in 1953-54 and to the UN Mission to the Congo in 1962.  As a rifle regiment it uses a bugle horn as its insignia - the same as the British Light Division, but unlike its British counterparts, marches in the same march pace used in the Indian Army as a whole.

Army Day (India) | Army Day is celebrated on 15 January every year in India, in recognition of Lieutenant General K. M. Cariappa's taking over as the first Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army from General Sir Francis Butcher, the last British Commander-in-Chief of India, on 15 January 1949.  The day is celebrated in the form of parades and other military shows in the national capital New Delhi as well as in all headquarters.  On 15 January 2017 India celebrated its 69th Indian Army day in New Delhi.  Army Day marks a day to salute the valiant soldiers who sacrificed their lives to protect the country and the people living in it.

Hindustan Field Force | The Hindustan Field Force was the first operational regiment of the Indian National Army that was formed in September 1942 under the first INA.  Under the command of J.K. Bhonsle, the unit was formed at Singapore and comprised three battalions derived from troops of the 17th Dogra Regiment, Garhwal Rifles and the 14th Punjab Regiment (now part of the Pakistan Army) and had a strength of nearly 2000 troops.
response.summary_2: The army is the Indian National Army (INA), formed in 1942 (Indian National Army [passage 1] — “formed… in 1942”). The First INA was “formally proclaimed in April 1942” (First Indian National Army [passage 3]). Subhas Brigade (passage 2) was formed in 1943 and the Hindustan Field Force (passage 7) was formed in September 1942; passages [1] and [3] explicitly support the 1942 formation and [2],[7] supply unit-level date metadata.
